title: Lonely Element in Array

content.problemStatement:
You are given a **sorted array of integers** AAA of size NNN. In this array, **every element occurs exactly twice**, except for **one element which occurs only once**.

Find and print the element that occurs **only once**.

Your solution should run in O(logN)O(log N)O(logN) time and use O(1)O(1)O(1) extra memory.

Function DeclarationFunction NamefindSinglefindSinglefindSingle – This function finds the unique element that appears only once in a sorted array.

Parameters
numsnumsnums : A sorted array of integers where every element appears exactly twice except one.
Return Value
Returns a single integer — the element that occurs only once.
Constraints
1≤N≤1051 \leq N \leq 10^51≤N≤105
0≤nums[i]≤1050 \leq nums[i] \leq 10^50≤nums[i]≤105
Array is sorted in non-decreasing order
Exactly **one** element appears once; all others appear exactly twice

Input Format

The first line contains a single integer NNN — the size of the array.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers representing the array elements.


Output Format

Print a single integer — the element that occurs only once.
constraints: Array is sorted in non‑decreasing order
Exactly one element appears once; all others appear exactly twice
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an odd integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 100000) – the size of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers in non‑decreasing order. Exactly one element appears once; all other elements appear exactly twice.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single integer – the element that occurs only once.

sampleTestCases.explanation: In the first test case the only element without a pair is 5. In the second test case the unpaired element is 8.
Array of size one, the single element is trivially the answer.
The unique element is at the beginning of the array.

solution.approach: Because the array is sorted and every element except one appears exactly twice, the pairs are adjacent. In a correct prefix before the unique element, the first occurrence of each pair is at an even index and the second at the next odd index. After the unique element this pattern is broken: the first occurrence of each pair shifts to an odd index. Using binary search we locate the first index where the pair property fails. At each step we force the middle index to be even; if nums[mid]==nums[mid+1] the unique element lies to the right, otherwise it lies at mid or to the left. The loop converges to the unique element in O(log N) time with O(1) extra space.

code.Java: import java.io.*;import java.util.*;public class Main{private static int findSingle(int[] nums){int left=0,right=nums.length-1;while(left<right){int mid=left+(right-left)/2;if((mid&1)==1)mid--;if(nums[mid]==nums[mid+1]){left=mid+2;}else{right=mid;}}return nums[left];}public static void main(String[] args)throws Exception{BufferedReader br=new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(System.in));StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();int T=Integer.parseInt(br.readLine().trim());while(T-- >0){int n=Integer.parseInt(br.readLine().trim());int[] arr=new int[n];StringTokenizer st=new StringTokenizer(br.readLine());for(int i=0;i<n;i++)arr[i]=Integer.parseInt(st.nextToken());sb.append(findSingle(arr)).append('\n');}System.out.print(sb.toString());}}
